issues.description: Path validation (backslash, absolute, traversal, .md extension, stem) is applied to ALL /start requests, including legacy callers that previously had no such validation. The spec says FR-3c: 'No new validation needed; FR-2a just reuses the existing check.' But the prior review rounds established that no such existing check exists. Adding validation is the right security call, but this is a behavioral change for legacy callers (NFR-1 says 'No schema migration' and existing callers 'keep working via FR-2b'). A legacy caller sending e.g. 'specs/README.txt' would now get 400 instead of a normal flow. This is arguably an improvement but technically an NFR-1 surface area concern.
issues.suggestion: Acknowledge this is intentional hardening. If strict NFR-1 backward compat is required, gate the new validation behind `spec_from_local` (apply only when spec_content is present). Otherwise, document this as a deliberate tightening of input validation.

issues.description: When no engineer credentials exist in the DB (no _name/_email rows), the fallback defaults to `req.engineer` for name and `{engineer}@nautiloop.dev` for email. This is reasonable, but the spec says 'The spec commit inherits the engineer's identity (the same name/email already used for implement/revise commits)'. If the loop engine's implement/revise stage uses a different mechanism to resolve identity, the spec commit attribution could differ from subsequent commits. There's no validation that credentials actually exist before proceeding.
issues.suggestion: Consider whether missing credentials should be an error (require `nemo auth` first) or if the fallback is acceptable. At minimum, log a warning when falling back to synthetic identity.

issues.description: The spec commit block (write_file_as + get_branch_sha) does not clean up the git branch on failure. If write_file_as or get_branch_sha fails, the loop is marked Failed in the DB, but the orphan git branch remains on the remote. Subsequent retries with the same spec content will generate the same branch name (content hash), and create_branch will fail or conflict. The existing create_branch error path also doesn't clean up — this is a pre-existing issue, but the local-spec path adds more failure points between branch creation and successful completion.
issues.suggestion: Add branch cleanup (delete_branch) in the error paths after create_branch succeeds but write_file_as or get_branch_sha fails, or document that orphan branches are expected and handled by a separate cleanup process.

issues.description: spec_content is always sent in the JSON body, even though spec_content is Option<String> on the server. The build_start_body function unconditionally includes 'spec_content' in the JSON. This means CLI callers can never exercise the legacy fallback path (FR-2b) via the CLI. The spec says FR-2b preserves behavior 'when spec_content is absent' — but the CLI will always send it. This is fine for the CLI (FR-1a mandates local-file-first), but it means the FR-2b legacy path is only reachable via direct API calls, which is the intent per spec.
issues.suggestion: No change needed — this matches the spec intent. The CLI always sends local content; FR-2b is for direct API callers.

issues.description: The author_name and author_email values are interpolated directly into git -c arguments via format!(). If an engineer's stored name or email contains shell metacharacters or git config injection sequences (e.g., newlines, semicolons), this could produce unexpected git behavior. Command::new + .args() prevents shell injection, but git -c values with newlines could still cause issues.
issues.suggestion: Validate or sanitize author_name and author_email to reject control characters and newlines before passing to git -c.

issues.description: generate_branch_name takes &spec_content (a reference to a String that may be up to 1 MB). This is fine — it's a reference, no copy. But spec_content as a String lives until end of function scope even after it's no longer needed (after write_file_as call). Minor memory efficiency concern for a 1 MB cap.
issues.suggestion: No change needed — the 1 MB cap makes this negligible.
